Tonight's pick for the South Bay 🌸

Local artist Kenneth Tan is leading a free patterned flower drawing workshop at Los Altos Library — and it sounds like exactly the kind of thing you'd be glad you went to. It starts at 6:30 PM, it's totally beginner-friendly, and you'll learn to draw a flower using simple line patterns inspired by traditional techniques. No art experience required. Just you, a pen, and a quiet weeknight.

Free. Open to adults and teens. At @SCCLD in Los Altos.

cards.name: Bagel Street Cafe
cards.address: 346 W Maude Ave, Sunnyvale, CA 94086, USA
cards.blurb: Bagel Street Cafe is the move for an easy, no-fuss morning. Grab a bagel sandwich and a coffee and ease into the day.
cards.why: Solid breakfast under $15 and they're already well into their morning by the time you show up.

cards.name: Sunnyvale Bay Trail
cards.address: 293-327 W Caribbean Dr, Sunnyvale, CA 94089, USA
cards.blurb: The Sunnyvale Bay Trail is a flat, scenic walk along the water with big sky views. Great morning light out here on a cool day like this.
cards.why: Perfect weather to stretch your legs before the temperature climbs.

cards.name: Rene Rose FILIPINO KITCHEN
cards.address: 621 Caliente Dr, Sunnyvale, CA 94085, USA
cards.blurb: Rene Rose Filipino Kitchen is a hidden gem in Sunnyvale. Try the local favorites and don't sleep on whatever the daily special is.
cards.why: Under $15 a head for food this good is a straight-up deal.

cards.name: Mai Do - Fine Stationery & Goods - Santana Row
cards.address: 378 Santana Row #1125, San Jose, CA 95128, USA
cards.blurb: Mai Do at Santana Row is a beautiful little stationery and goods shop worth browsing slowly. Pick up a card, a notebook, something nice.
cards.why: It's the kind of shop that makes you realize you need things you didn't know existed.

cards.name: Shoup Park
cards.address: 400 University Ave, Los Altos, CA 94022, USA
cards.blurb: Shoup Park in Los Altos is a lovely tree-lined park with a creek and plenty of shaded spots to wander or just sit. Good afternoon energy here.
cards.why: A Thursday afternoon here feels genuinely peaceful.

cards.name: Nick the Greek
cards.address: 2002 El Camino Real, Santa Clara, CA 95050, USA
cards.blurb: Nick the Greek on El Camino is fast, filling, and genuinely good. Go for the gyro or the bowls and grab a seat inside.
cards.why: Casual dinner that hits every time without overthinking it.

cards.name: Summer reading for all ages
cards.address: 485 Lasuen Mall, Stanford, CA 94305
cards.blurb: Pick up your next favorite book and beat the summer reading slide at the library.
cards.why: A solid pick to break up the day.

DESIGN DIRECTION:
Linocut / block print aesthetic. Bold carved-looking shapes with visible texture in the inked areas, rough uneven edges, high contrast. Two-to-three color palette: ink black plus one or two warm accents (brick red, mustard yellow, forest green). Handmade, tactile, indie-press feel. Chunky hand-carved sans-serif headlines.

COMPOSITION DIRECTION (interpret within the style above):
Layout: centered axis — all content anchored to a vertical center line, symmetrical feel, clean spine running through.
Typography: primary face is clean sans-serif, but the lead word/phrase is hand-lettered in a loose script for a personal touch.
Decoration: soft organic blob shapes in the background behind text sections, faintly suggesting landscapes.
Density: packed and dense — information fills most of the frame, very little negative space.
Mood: electric and vibrant — push saturation, add movement and visual punch.

tonight-pick.imagePrompt: Risograph screen print illustration in 4:5 portrait ratio. NO PEOPLE, NO TEXT. Grainy 3-color overprint (electric teal, burnt sienna, cream) with heavy halftone texture and registration shift. Dense editorial composition with wide margins; torn paper and washi tape strips creating jagged section boundaries throughout. Abstract fragmented game die and stacked card silhouettes woven into chaotic color fields and diagonal stripe patterns. Zine energy—elements colliding, intentional imperfect overlaps, organic torn edges. Bold graphic energy over polish.

cards.name: Shoreline Park & Lake
cards.address: Shoreline at Mountain View
cards.blurb: Walk or bike the lakeside loop, watch the windsurfers set up on the water, and soak in the cool morning air before the day heats up.
cards.why: A quick, easy outdoor stretch right in Mountain View makes for a refreshing start before you head into a full day of culture and food.

cards.name: Triton Museum of Art
cards.address: 1505 Warburton Ave, Santa Clara, CA 95050, USA
cards.blurb: Wander through the galleries and don't skip the sculpture garden out back. The contemporary rotating exhibits are worth slowing down for.
cards.why: Free admission and genuinely good art makes this an easy yes on a Friday morning.

cards.name: Los Gatos Creek Trail
cards.address: 1250 Dell Ave, Campbell, CA 95008, USA
cards.blurb: Walk the creekside trail and shake off the wine a little. The path is flat, shaded in spots, and genuinely relaxing at this pace.
cards.why: A mellow walk after an afternoon winery is the right move before a big evening.

cards.name: Smoking Pig BBQ
cards.address: 1144 N 4th St, San Jose, CA 95112, USA
cards.blurb: Pull up to the counter and order the brisket plate. Get the mac and cheese on the side. Eat all of it.
cards.why: This is some of the best BBQ in the South Bay and a Friday dinner here never disappoints.

cards.name: Candlelight: The Best of Hans Zimmer
cards.address: 101 Paseo De San Antonio, San Jose
cards.blurb: Listen to Hans Zimmer film scores by candlelight orchestra.
cards.why: It's a genuinely cool way to end a Friday night and the atmosphere in that room is something else.

cards.name: House of Bagels
cards.address: 1712-D Miramonte Ave, Mountain View, CA 94040, USA
cards.blurb: Start the morning at House of Bagels in Mountain View. Grab a fresh bagel with your spread of choice and a coffee while the day warms up.
cards.why: It's a classic South Bay breakfast move and they're open early.

cards.name: The Dish Trail
cards.address: Stanford Foothills (Stanford Ave trailhead)
cards.blurb: Hit The Dish Trail from the Stanford Ave trailhead. It's a steady climb with sweeping views of the Bay and foothills — worth pushing to the top before the afternoon crowd shows up.
cards.why: High 68°F is perfect hiking weather and the views from the ridge are genuinely great.

cards.name: Cantor Arts Center
cards.address: Cantor Arts Center, Stanford University
cards.blurb: Walk through Cantor Arts Center on Stanford's campus. The Rodin sculptures in the outdoor garden are a highlight, and the rotating galleries inside are always worth a look.
cards.why: Free world-class art right on campus, and it's a natural next stop after the hike.

cards.name: Oren's Hummus
cards.address: 261 University Ave, Palo Alto, CA 94301, USA
cards.blurb: Head to Oren's Hummus on University Ave for lunch. Order the hummus plate, add a side or two, and eat well without overthinking it.
cards.why: It's one of the best lunch values on the Peninsula and you'll leave satisfied.

cards.name: Restaurant Gish
cards.address: 1998 Homestead Rd #113, Santa Clara, CA 95050, USA
cards.blurb: Drive over to Restaurant Gish in Santa Clara for dinner. The menu skews creative and the kitchen takes it seriously, so try the local favorite dishes and let the evening slow down.
cards.why: It punches above its weight for a Saturday night dinner.

cards.name: Art of Science 2026
cards.address: 475 Via Ortega, Stanford, CA 94305
cards.blurb: Browse submissions celebrating the visual beauty of scientific research from campus departments.
cards.why: A solid pick to break up the day.

Boomerang and starburst atomic-era shapes scattered throughout. Generous negative space. Bold saturated palette with electric visual punch, clean mid-century curves, zero psychedelic effects.

DESIGN DIRECTION:
Art Deco travel poster aesthetic (1930s golden age). Bold geometric sunbursts, stepped triangular motifs, streamlined chrome-style lines. Deep teal, burnt orange, cream, and gold accents. Elegant Art Deco sans-serif type with condensed letterforms. Hint of travel poster nostalgia meets modern city guide.

COMPOSITION DIRECTION (interpret within the style above):
Layout: asymmetric — elements intentionally off-grid, some stops float left, others right, varied sizes.
Typography: monospace typewriter-style face for times and metadata, a contrasting display face for venue names.
Decoration: collage-feel scraps of torn paper, washi tape strips, or ripped edges around section boundaries.
Density: intentionally asymmetric — dense on one side, airy on the other.
Mood: electric and vibrant — push saturation, add movement and visual punch.

CRITICAL: All text must be spelled correctly and fully legible. This is a graphic design poster, NOT a photograph. The text content is the star — make it beautiful but readable. Do NOT include "STOP 1", "STOP 2" labels — just the times and venue names.
